data.name: Gregor Kobel
data.description: After a long wait, Kobel has finally become Switzerland’s first-choice goalkeeper following Yann Sommer’s retirement in the autumn of 2024. For the national team he has not yet quite managed to replicate his outstanding form at Borussia Dortmund and it was not until his 13th international that the Zurich-born keeper managed to keep his first clean sheet. Kobel may be the most valuable player in the Swiss squad – according to Transfermarkt – and is an important leader in the squad but he doesn’t like the spotlight and rarely finds himself creating headlines. As a teenager, Kobel was so multitalented he aspired to a career in tennis, snowboarding or ice hockey as well as football.

data.name: Miro Muheim
data.description: Moved from FC Zürich to Chelsea as a 16-year-old and, while he did not make it all the way to the first team, he has some tremendous memories from his time in London. “You become more mature by moving abroad on your own and the setup was fantastic. I remember Eden Hazard suddenly being in the changing room as he was playing for the under-23s while returning to fitness. He was so nice and relaxed. And what a player. He showed straight away what you can do with a ball: simply sensational.” Returned to Switzerland and St Gallen in 2018 and made the breakthrough despite having suffered an anterior cruciate ligament injury early on. Joined Hamburg three years later and won his first Switzerland cap as a 2. Bundesliga player before being promoted to the top flight. Is now part of the leadership group at HSV.

data.name: Silvan Widmer
data.description: Back in form after a difficult spell for country and club, the attacking full-back regained his starting place for both during the 2025-26 season. “Of course, last season was disappointing for me,” he said. “But I’m not the sort of person who harbours frustration. I never had to force myself to get going; I always enjoyed being part of the team and, as a result, was also a role model for others who were going through a difficult time.” He is a very reliable player but opponents of the highest calibre can cause him problems, as was the case in the 4-3 defeat against Germany in March. A captain at Mainz, he is not afraid to discuss sensitive topics and said that if he had to step in as skipper during the World Cup in Qatar he would definitely wear the rainbow armband and he has also said he would never play in Saudi Arabia. While injured at Mainz in 2023, he said: “I get to spend more time at home and there I do whatever is needed, whether that is taking packages to the post office or helping my wife, Céline, with her own company.”

data.name: Nico Elvedi
data.description: The Zurich-born player with roots in Graubünden moved from FC Zürich to Mönchengladbach as a teenager in 2015 – and is still there. There have been talks of a move, but it never materialised and he is now, in fact, the club’s all-time leading appearance-holder in the Bundesliga.  Nicknamed Eisvogel (Kingfisher) In Germany after a cool and composed debut against Bayern Munich of all teams. Nico’s twin brother, Jan, is also a professional footballer and was most recently on loan from Kaiserslautern to Greuther Fürth. Jan is full of praise for his brother, although thinks he may be a little bit “too nice” on the pitch. “Nico is an all-rounder and has practically no weaknesses,” says Jan. “But perhaps he could be a bit more aggressive and use his elbows a bit more.” The brothers’ greatest wish: to play against each other one day. Elvedi has made a strong comeback for the national team after a difficult year when he lost his place in the starting lineup for Euro 2024. He was among the best Switzerland players in the World Cup qualifiers.

data.name: Manuel Akanji
data.description: Has developed into a world-class defender at Manchester City and Inter and, together with Granit Xhaka, an undisputed leader for the national team. His father is from Nigeria and his middle name, Obafemi, means “beloved of the king”, which is why he had a crown tattooed on his left forearm. Another tattoo – “Prove them wrong” – was inked after doubts that he would make it all the way while with the FC Winterthur youth teams. He even overcame an anterior cruciate ligament injury at Basel to continue his rise to the top. At the start of 2018, aged 22, he moved to Borussia Dortmund for £23.5m and four years later to Manchester City, where he won the treble in 2023. Akanji is also good with numbers; he once wowed the whole of Switzerland with his mental arithmetic skills on a TV show. The video went viral.

data.name: Denis Zakaria
data.description: Borussia Mönchengladbach, Juventus, Chelsea and now Monaco. How to keep up with Denis Zakaria? Hardly any Swiss player has represented as many prestigious clubs as him, yet with the national team it has been complicated at times. The Monaco captain has had trouble finding his place under Murat Yakin, mainly because it is not easy getting picked when you have Granit Xhaka and Remo Freuler in central midfield. When Zakaria was called upon because Xhaka was missing – such as the Euro 2020 quarter-final against Spain or in September 2024 against the same opponents in the Nations League – he often seemed out of place in the game. Now, however, Yakin may have found the right role for Zakaria: as a right-sided player in a defensive back three, which he played for the first time in the friendly against Norway in March. “It was a successful performance from Denis,” said Yakin. He is one of the leaders in the national team squad setup and acts as the big brother of the large Geneva contingent in the squad. In 2019, in an interview with Blick, he admitted to making some errors when he was younger. “I bought a really expensive car once but I then realised it was a mistake. It brings nothing. So I sold it.”

data.name: Breel Embolo
data.description: Breel Embolo has undergone an astonishing transformation in recent years. The forward, who had always stood out for his impressive physicality, has now added more goals to his repertoire. At the time of writing he has scored eight times for Switzerland since the start of 2025 – the same number of goals he had in the previous six years for his country. But it is not only on the pitch that the striker, born in Cameroon but raised in Switzerland, has matured. In the past, Embolo has had a tendency to cause controversy. In early 2021, he attended an illegal party despite the Covid lockdown and hid in a bathtub when the police arrived. In 2023, he was convicted of making multiple threats. His comeback at Monaco was derailed because he allegedly obtained two fake Covid certificates in 2021. Today, however, the man from Basel is a key leader within the team, part of the captaincy group and, thanks to his bilingualism, an important link between the German- and French-speaking players.

data.name: Remo Freuler
data.description: Has come a long way since those dark days when he failed to make his breakthrough at Grasshopper, despite scoring a goal in the derby against FC Zürich at the age of 18. He first went on loan to the Challenge League side Winterthur before catching the eye at Lucerne. In January 2016 he joined Atalanta, where, despite many telling him the move abroad came too early, he became an instant hit. He spent five years in Bergamo, being a crucial part of Gian Piero Gasperini’s successful time at the club, gaining Champions League experience along the way. After a year with Nottingham Forest in England he returned to Bologna, where he was part of the side who ended a 50-year trophy drought by winning the Coppa Italia. Freuler is this season chasing down Stephan Lichtsteiner as the Swiss player with most Serie A appearances and is a leader within the national-team setup. “When I don’t like something, I don’t stay quiet, I say something,” he told watson.ch. “If you don’t do that you are a little grey mouse. That is something that has changed in recent years. But at the end what you do on the pitch is always the most important thing. Actions always speak louder than words.”

data.name: Johan Manzambi
data.description: The big discovery of the past 12 months. After he burst on to the scene from seemingly nowhere at Freiburg, Murat Yakin called him up for the summer friendlies in 2025 – and Manzambi responded by scoring and assisting on his first start, a 4-0 win against the USA. The technically gifted player can play in any position and at Freiburg he is often deployed as a defensive or attacking midfielder while he has often appeared on the wing for his country. Has matured since moving from the French-speaking part of Switzerland to Germany at the age of 17 but can still lose his cool on the pitch, and was sent off twice in Bundesliga matches during the 2025-26 season. After Freiburg had beaten Basel 2-1 in the Europa League last September, Xherdan Shaqiri gatecrashed an interview with Manzambi and said: “It was good to see him play, he has a big future ahead of him.”

data.name: Granit Xhaka
data.description: Captain, record-breaker and the manager’s right-hand man: Granit Xhaka is the undisputed leader of the national team at his seventh major tournament – both on and off the pitch. In the March friendlies against Germany and Norway, the captain was rested for three-quarters of the two matches. “We have to be prepared to play without him at times, or when he is marked man-to-man, as he was against Germany,” said Murat Yakin. After a difficult start to their relationship, the pair are now very close. The midfielder has been around for some time now. In 2011, Ottmar Hitzfeld threw the then 18-year-old Xhaka in at the deep end at Wembley during a Euro qualifier against England and the Under-17 World Cup winner is now the record holder for international caps in his country with more than 140 appearances. Many thought his career was on the way down when he joined Bayer Leverkusen in 2023 after seven years at Arsenal but it was the opposite – and as Xabi Alonso’s leader on the pitch he took the club to a first ever Bundesliga title, won the German Cup and reached the Europa League final. Now back in England, he says: “It’s important for me to experience moments of suffering and solidarity too.” His parents are from Kosovo; his father, Ragip, was arrested in 1986 at the age of 22 during a demonstration against the communist central government.

data.name: Dan Ndoye
data.description: Had his breakthrough at Euro 2024 and, after the 3-1 victory against Hungary, he and his father, Saliou, FaceTimed with Dan’s grandmother Bineta, who lives in Senegal and is cheering on her grandson from there. “I keep getting photos from Senegal. People there follow the Swiss national team’s matches regularly,” says Ndoye, who often goes on holiday to the west coast of Africa. Family plays a very important role in the life of the Lausanne native, who has a younger brother and a younger sister. Last summer he became the second most expensive Swiss player ever behind Granit Xhaka as Nottingham Forest paid about £35m to sign him from Bologna. It has been a stop-start first season for the winger in England but he is a guaranteed starter for Switzerland thanks to his speed and dribbling ability. “I am very ambitious and know what I can do,” he said in October. “I am someone who works hard and wants to develop – that is why I am in the Premier League today. That doesn’t mean that I am happy, though, I will always want to go higher.”

data.name: Yvon Mvogo
data.description: The Cameroon-born goalkeeper is the perfect second-choice. He fits in well with the team, accepts his role and is there when needed. "He has always been in a good mood, with a smile on his face – a real fan favourite," his coach at Young Boys, Uli Forte, once said. He has impressed in virtually all his 12 international appearances, keeping six clean sheets. His club career has not always followed a smooth path, however. In 2017, when he was regarded as one of the most talented goalkeepers on the continent, he left YB but failed to establish himself at RB Leipzig. Via PSV Eindhoven, he ended up at Lorient, where he turned down offers from Parma and Celtic before getting relegated last summer. It looked as if he was going to leave the French club but ended up re-signing. As a child, church service was compulsory for him on Sundays. “I didn’t fancy it, as it was always my only day off,” he says with a smile. “But my mother told me me that it’s important to thank God.”

data.name: Ricardo Rodriguez
data.description: Alongside the captain, Granit Xhaka, Rodriguez is the player with the most caps in the history of Swiss football. Xhaka is also his best friend in the national team and the two used to share a room. Together they won the Under-17 World Cup in Nigeria back in 2009. The historic success launched Rodriguez’s career, taking him to Wolfsburg, Milan, PSV Eindhoven, Torino and now Real Betis. The son of a Spanish father and a Chilean mother, he grew up in the working-class Zurich neighbourhood of Schwamendingen, but struggled at school, which is why his mother, Marcela, was very concerned about him. Marcela, sadly, died of cancer in 2016. His brothers, Roberto and Francisco, also made it as professional footballers, but Ricardo is the most successful of the three. Rodriguez had a difficult start in life: due to a diaphragmatic hernia at birth, doctors gave him only a 50% per cent chance of survival.

data.name: Ardon Jashari
data.description: A big discussion point in the national team. Jashari is regarded as the most talented midfielder in Switzerland and a possible successor to Granit Xhaka but game time has so far been limited, with the Sunderland player and Remo Freuler often preferred to the 24-year-old. Has improved immeasurably since joining Club Brugge in 2024 and is now with Milan. In 2025 he told Sky about his early years in Switzerland. “I grew up in Neuheim next to Swiss families who had a higher social standard as well as Albanian ones, who lived more modestly. The feeling of this difference was painful at times as I had the feeling that I had to look up to the wealthier people, as if I were worth less. But my parents did everything to minimise this difference and to make sure that I was not affected by this social inequality. They worked extremely hard to make sure that we had the same opportunities as the others.”

data.name: Djibril Sow
data.description: The midfielder is arriving at the World Cup with a spring in his step after one of the best seasons of his career. Especially in front of goal he has displayed a new, improved level of maturity. For the national team, however, it is a different story as, despite winning more than 50 caps, he does not have a clear role under Murat Yakin. He was a starter at the last World Cup but has not been called upon in the past 12 months, partly because of a toe injury. “Djibbi” can play as a No 6 and a No 8 – and he knows how to win titles. In 2018, he was part of the YB side that won the league for the first time in 32 years. And in 2022, he secured the Europa League with Eintracht Frankfurt.

data.name: Christian Fassnacht
data.description: The biggest surprise in Murat Yakin’s squad. Last autumn, Fassnacht returned to the national setup after a two-and-a-half-year absence but was again missing from the squad this March. With 18 goals, he was the top scorer in the Swiss Super League in 2025-26 and he is a true team player. “The statistics don’t lie; he knows where the goal is,” said Yakin. Fassnacht’s finest hour came in the Euro 2020 Last 16, when he won possession for both Switzerland’s late goals in a 3-3 draw with France before victory on penalties. The striker was once dropped from the FC Zürich youth team, forcing him to spend several years in amateur football before making the leap to the top flight via Winterthur, Thun and then Young Boys. In Berne, he has since become a legend. His spell abroad at Norwich was not a success, so he returned in 2025 after a year and a half.

data.name: Rubén Vargas
data.description: In early 2025, after five and a half years at Augsburg, Vargas moved to La Liga and Sevilla. There, the Lucerne native has been a regular starter when fit and the same goes for Switzerland. He is known as the unofficial favourite player of the national-team coach, Murat Yakin. One of Switzerland’s heroes against Italy in the last 16-game at Euro 2024, scoring the second goal of a 2-0 victory with a stunning long-range strike. The son of a Dominican father and a Swiss mother, he completed a three-year apprenticeship as a painter during his time at Lucerne. His father is a golf instructor and Vargas can often be seen on the course as well. In the national team, he has a particularly close friendship with Noah Okafor.

data.name: Eray Cömert
data.description: Endeared himself to the Valencia faithful in February with some lively celebrations after the derby win against Levante. After the final whistle Cömert put his shirt on the corner flag and celebrated exuberantly in front of the fans in images that went viral. Levante fans were less impressed and pelted him with objects as he made his way down the tunnel, with a half-full plastic bottle hitting Cömert on the head. He was unrepentant. “I’ve no problem with that at all,” he said. “Emotions are part and parcel of football, all the more so in a derby.” Joined Valencia in 2002 but it is not until this season that he managed to establish himself as a starter following two loan spells (at Nantes and Real Valladolid). Was up until 2018 playing as Eray Cümart after a mistake by passport officials – affecting the whole family – but was belatedly able to change it to the correct spelling.

data.name: Noah Okafor
data.description: At the start of the year it seemed unthinkable that Okafor would travel to the World Cup. Things had been bad since the Euro 2024, where he did not play a single minute but is said to have displayed a lack of team spirit and been bad-tempered. He then proceeded to meet the national-team director, Pierluigi Tami, without telling the coach, Murat Yakin. Finally, after being left out of the squad for the World Cup qualifiers, he criticised Yakin in an interview. Remarkably, though, a visit by Yakin to Leeds seems to have solved the problem and Okafor was included for the March friendlies. There he apologised to the team, which is good news for Switzerland because he has qualities that no other Swiss striker has at the moment.

data.name: Michel Aebischer
data.description: A man for the big moment. After being mainly a substitute for Switzerland he grabbed his chance at Euro 2024 and produced his best performance yet for his country in the opening game against Hungary, scoring a goal and providing an assist. What wasn’t known at the time was that he was playing through the pain because of a groin injury. In the end he had to have an operation and lost his place at Bologna and with the national team. In the summer of 2025 he therefore joined Pisa. “I no longer had the role I wanted,” he said. “Because I’m someone who likes to get plenty of playing time and also plays an important role in the team. That’s why I deliberately took a step back.” Fluent in all three official Swiss languages – Italian, German and French – he responded diplomatically when asked if he preferred Italian or Swiss food: “You are not going to struggle to find nice food in Italy, that’s for sure,” before adding that he was missing a good fondue.

data.name: Marvin Keller
data.description: Keller is "a cool sock" according to one of his former Young Boys teammates, Fabian Rieder, “as a person and as a goalkeeper”. That is Swiss-German for being calm and collected. He can be loud on the pitch, though. “Maybe that is because he is from Zurich,” a current teammate, Sandro Lauper, suggests. Born in London when his father was working there, Keller grew up and came through the youth teams at Grasshopper in Switzerland. He is regarded as one of Switzerland’s most promising goalkeeping talents and, in the past two years, has fought his way up from the second division to replace David von Ballmoos at the serial champions Young Boys. He may well be on the move in the summer and has said: “I want to play in a top-five league.”  Played at the under-21 Euros but has yet to make his senior international debut at the time of writing.

data.name: Fabian Rieder
data.description: “You have to take more responsibility,” Rieder said in 2023 in an interview with Swiss television when asked about his father dying when the player was only 11. “You are confronted by things you didn’t expect. But I also have to make clear that I had a good childhood. It did mean, though, that I was probably a bit more mature than others, personality wise." The national-team coach, Murat Yakin, is a big fan of the tireless worker who often leads the press for the Swiss. In 2023, he left the serial champions Young Boys for France and Rennes in a deal worth £14.2m. It didn’t work out for him there, however, and after an equally hit-and-miss spell on loan at Stuttgart, he joined Augsburg in the summer of 2025.

data.name: Zeki Amdouni
data.description: The son of a Turkish kiosk owner and a Tunisian mother is still regarded as one of the country’s greatest talents, but he has faced a race against time to recover from an anterior cruciate ligament injury suffered in pre-season with Burnley. Bayern Munich’s Vincent Kompany is an admirer and it was the Belgian who took Amdouni to Burnley in 2023. The Switzerland manager, Murat Yakin, also thinks highly of a player whose style is that of a street footballer from Geneva, not dissimilar to that of Xherdan Shaqiri. Yakin invited him to be part of the squad for the March friendlies despite his not being match-fit yet. In an interview with Blick he explained why he now prefers Lionel Messi to Cristiano Ronaldo. “When I was young I liked everything about Ronaldo; his hair, his boots, his whole style. When I turned 13, though, and really started to understand football there was only one player for me: Messi.” He also explained why he has adopted a bow-and-arrow goal celebration. “It was something between me and a friend,” he said. “It comes from a French rapper called Jul, he sings about an arrow in one of his songs so I said to the friend that from then on I was going to do that as a celebration. I wanted to do something no one else does.”

data.name: Aurèle Amenda
data.description: Earlier this season Amenda’s career was going nowhere. He was mainly a substitute under Dino Toppmöller at Eintracht Frankfurt and there was talk of a winter move, but a change of manager, to Albert Riera, had a transformative effect for the man from Biel. He has become a regular starter in the Bundesliga and the national-team coach, Murat Yakin, was already a fan of the physical defender. In fact he sees him as a future successor to Manuel Akanji. “The move did not work out the way I had envisaged,” Amenda told Frankfurter Rundschau in March. “But I stayed positive and mentally strong, that helped me a lot.” The Swiss-Cameroonian dual national came through the youth ranks at Young Boys, winning two league titles and one Swiss Cup with the club before making the leap to the Bundesliga at the age of 21.

data.name: Luca Jaquez
data.description: “At first I didn’t like it all,” Jaquez told blue.ch in 2024 when asked about the time when the Lucerne legend Michel Renggli, the club's under-18s coach, had moved him from an attacking role to central defence. “I had always dreamed about a career as a forward player. It took me a few weeks before I realised that I could have a future at the back.” He credits his mother and sister for his becoming a professional footballer: “My mum is everything for me. Since I was young she has always been by my side and supported me at every tournament, every game. I don’t know how I could have become a footballer without her.” And his sister, who had to stop gymnastics because Jaquez’s football took over and their mum couldn’t drive them to both? “At the time I was so focused on football I am not sure I noticed. But looking back maybe that wasn’t so fair.” Was due to make his debut for the senior national team in November 2024 but Murat Yakin ended up not being able to select Jaquez as he had been sent off for the under-21s a few weeks earlier. A regular at Stuttgart despite fierce competition because of his superb buildup play, he is the most talented Swiss defender coming through the ranks.

data.name: Cedric Itten
data.description: “The most important thing is the passion and joy of playing football,” Itten said when asked how he has made it as a professional footballer. “Of course you have to have some talent but, above all, hard work. The goal has to be to improve every single day, that was the key to my success.” During the 2025-26 season he has been the most prolific Swiss striker abroad, although the fact that he has been playing in the second tier in Germany for Fortuna Düsseldorf counts against him. The former Rangers players – he won the title in Scotland – was not part of the squad for the March friendlies but worked hard to get included in the travelling party to the World Cup. He scored six minutes into his international debut and has, at the time of writing, a good record of five goals in 13 games for Switzerland. This will be his first major tournament for his country.
